unit.name: Why vision matters: evolution, sensing, and human attempts to build seeing machines
unit.description: Motivates vision as central to intelligence through evolutionary history and early imaging devices, linking biological sensing to the computational challenge of visual intelligence.
unit.summary: The lecture traces the history of vision back to the Cambrian explosion and argues that the onset of photosensitive sensing changed life from passive metabolism into active interaction with the environment [ts=199s] [ts=283s]. Li argues that the evolution of vision is tied to the evolution of intelligence itself, noting that most animals rely on vision and that humans devote more than half of cortical cells to visual processing [ts=338s] [ts=366s]. The segment then jumps to human civilization and camera obscura, using Leonardo da Vinci and earlier thinkers as examples of humanity's attempt to understand image formation and build machines that see [ts=381s] [ts=439s]. The key message is that cameras or eyes alone are not enough; understanding visual intelligence is the real problem [ts=442s] [ts=455s].

key_points.text: The Cambrian explosion is used as a motivating origin point for vision because simple light sensing changed survival and behavior.
Without sensors, life is described as passive metabolism; with sensors, organisms become active agents in the environment.
More than half of human cortical cells are involved in visual processing.
Cameras are apparatuses, but seeing requires understanding visual intelligence rather than just image capture.

kp_catalog.name: Visual intelligence and object recognition
kp_catalog.description: Object recognition is treated as a core capability of both human and machine vision and as the starting point of modern computer vision progress. The lecture frames vision as central to animal and human intelligence, not merely one application area inside AI.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because visual intelligence and object recognition formalizes how object recognition is treated as a core capability of both human and machine vision and as the starting point of modern computer vision progress, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: CS231n focuses on the intersection of deep learning and computer vision
kp_catalog.description: The class is explicitly scoped to the overlap between deep learning methods and core computer vision problems.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cS231n focuses on the intersection of deep learning and computer vision formalizes how the class is explicitly scoped to the overlap between deep learning methods and core computer vision problems, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
